title: PostgreSQL High Availability: Complete Guide with Patroni, ETCD & Multi-Port Setup
description: A comprehensive, step-by-step guide to building a production-grade PostgreSQL High Availability cluster using Patroni and ETCD. Learn to deploy multiple PostgreSQL instances on different ports, configure automatic failover, set up HAProxy load balancing, and run multiple HA clusters side by side with zero downtime.
author: E-Learning Platform
heroAlt: PostgreSQL High Availability architecture: 3-node Patroni cluster (node0 leader 192.168.68.200, node1 replica 192.168.68.201, node2 replica 192.168.68.202) with ETCD, HAProxy on node0, and application layer

sections.title: Introduction
content: Database downtime costs businesses millions every year. Whether you're running an e-commerce platform, a SaaS application, or a critical enterprise system, losing your database means losing customers, revenue, and trust. PostgreSQL High Availability (HA) solves this problem by ensuring your database remains accessible even when individual servers fail.

In this comprehensive guide, you'll learn how to build a production-grade PostgreSQL HA cluster using Patroni (the industry-standard HA manager) and ETCD (a distributed key-value store for leader election). We'll cover everything from architecture design to hands-on configuration, including running multiple PostgreSQL instances on different ports simultaneously.
By the end of this guide, you'll have a fully automated, self-healing PostgreSQL cluster that can survive server failures without manual intervention.

infoBox.content: This guide uses PostgreSQL 18, Patroni 4.x, ETCD 3.5, and HAProxy 2.x. All commands are tested on Ubuntu 22.04 LTS but work on Debian, RHEL, Rocky Linux, and CentOS with minimal adjustments.

sections.title: Architecture Overview
content: Before we dive into the setup, let's understand how the pieces fit together.

cards.title: PostgreSQL
cards.desc: The world's most advanced open-source relational database. Provides streaming replication for keeping replicas in sync with the primary.

cards.title: Patroni
cards.desc: A Python-based HA template for PostgreSQL. Automates leader election, replica management, and handles failover when the primary fails.

cards.title: ETCD
cards.desc: A distributed key-value store that acts as the source of truth. Stores cluster state, leader information, and coordinates decisions between Patroni nodes.

cards.title: HAProxy
cards.desc: A high-performance TCP/HTTP load balancer. Routes read/write traffic to the appropriate PostgreSQL node based on Patroni's REST API health checks.

sections.title: Architecture Diagram
content: The following architecture shows a 3-node PostgreSQL cluster managed by Patroni, with ETCD providing distributed consensus and HAProxy routing application traffic.

image.alt: PostgreSQL HA architecture with 3 nodes, ETCD cluster, HAProxy, and application layer

subsections.title: Data Flow Summary
subsections.content: Applications connect to HAProxy ports 5000 (write) and 5001 (read). HAProxy forwards writes to the Patroni leader and distributes reads across all replicas. Patroni monitors PostgreSQL health via its REST API and triggers automatic failover if the leader fails.

sections.title: Prerequisites
content: You need at least 3 Linux servers (physical or virtual) to build a high-availability cluster. A 3-node setup gives you reliable failover and tolerates the loss of any single node.

rows: Low latency is critical for replication

infoBox.content: ⚠️ BEFORE YOU START: Write down your 3 VM hostnames and IP addresses. You'll need them for every config below. In this guide we use: node0=192.168.68.200, node1=192.168.68.201, node2=192.168.68.202. Replace these with YOUR actual IPs — copy-pasting blindly is the #1 cause of failures.

items.label: Package Installation by Node - Quick Reference
items.text: The table below shows exactly which packages to install on each VM. All commands use `sudo apt install`.

rows: sudo apt install python3 python3-pip python3-venv libpq-dev -y

items.label: Hostname & IP Setup (DO THIS FIRST)
text: Your 3 VMs must be able to reach each other by hostname. Do this on EVERY node:
1. Check your current hostname: `hostnamectl`
2. Check your current IP: `ip addr show | grep 'inet '`
3. Verify all 3 VMs can ping each other: `ping -c 1 <other-vm-ip>`

items.label: /etc/hosts entries (add to ALL 3 nodes)
items.code:
sudo nano /etc/hosts

# Add these lines to all 3 nodes (replace IPs with yours):
192.168.68.200  node0.local  node0
192.168.68.201  node1.local  node1
192.168.68.202  node2.local  node2

items.label: Set hostname on each node (run separately on each VM)
items.code:
# On Node 0:
sudo hostnamectl set-hostname node0

# On Node 1:
sudo hostnamectl set-hostname node1

# On Node 2:
sudo hostnamectl set-hostname node2

# ⚠️ LOG OUT and log back in for hostname change to take effect
# Verify: hostnamectl | grep 'Static hostname'

items.label: Verify inter-VM connectivity (run on every node)
items.code:
# From node0, test both other nodes:
ping -c 2 node1.local
ping -c 2 node2.local

# From node1:
ping -c 2 node0.local
ping -c 2 node2.local

# From node2:
ping -c 2 node0.local
ping -c 2 node1.local

# Expected: 0% packet loss
# If ping fails, check /etc/hosts entries and firewall

sections.title: Part 1: ETCD Cluster Setup
content: ETCD is the distributed key-value store that Patroni uses as its Distributed Configuration Store (DCS). It's responsible for leader election, storing cluster state, and coordinating decisions between Patroni nodes. We'll install a 3-node ETCD cluster for high availability.
ETCD uses port 2379 for client communication and port 2380 for peer-to-peer communication between ETCD nodes.

subsections.title: ⭐ CRITICAL: How Ubuntu's ETCD Package Reads Config
content: This is the #1 mistake in ETCD setup. Ubuntu's apt package for ETCD reads configuration from /etc/default/etcd (environment variables), NOT from /etc/etcd/etcd.conf.yml. If you create a YAML file there, the service will IGNORE it and start with defaults (name='ubuntu', listen on 127.0.0.1 only).
You have two options:
Option A (Recommended): Set environment variables in /etc/default/etcd
Option B: Tell the service to use your YAML file via ETCD_OPTS in /etc/default/etcd
This guide uses Option A — it's simpler and is the standard Ubuntu approach.

infoBox.content: ⚠️ After installing ETCD with apt, it auto-starts with default config. You MUST stop it immediately and configure it properly before starting again. Running with defaults will cause cluster formation failures (shows 'Name: ubuntu' and binds to 127.0.0.1 only).

subsections.title: Step 1: Install ETCD on All Nodes
subsections.description: Install ETCD, then stop the auto-started default instance.

items.label: Run this on ALL 3 NODES
items.code:
# ---------- NODE 0 (192.168.68.200) ----------
node0:~$ sudo apt update
node0:~$ sudo apt install etcd -y
node0:~$ sudo systemctl stop etcd
node0:~$ sudo systemctl disable etcd
node0:~$ etcd --version

# ---------- NODE 1 (192.168.68.201) ----------
node1:~$ sudo apt update
node1:~$ sudo apt install etcd -y
node1:~$ sudo systemctl stop etcd
node1:~$ sudo systemctl disable etcd
node1:~$ etcd --version

# ---------- NODE 2 (192.168.68.202) ----------
node2:~$ sudo apt update
node2:~$ sudo apt install etcd -y
node2:~$ sudo systemctl stop etcd
node2:~$ sudo systemctl disable etcd
node2:~$ etcd --version

# Why stop & disable? apt auto-starts etcd with defaults (listen on 127.0.0.1 only).
# We need to configure it first, then start it properly.

items.label: Verify: Check which config file ETCD actually reads
items.code:
systemctl cat etcd

# Look for: EnvironmentFile=-/etc/default/etcd
# This confirms the service reads from /etc/default/etcd (not /etc/etcd/etcd.conf.yml)
# Output should show:
#   [Service]
#   EnvironmentFile=-/etc/default/etcd
#   ExecStart=/usr/bin/etcd $ETCD_OPTS

subsections.title: Step 2: Configure ETCD on Node 0 (192.168.68.200)
subsections.description: ⚠️ Replace the example IPs with your actual IPs. If you copy-paste blindly, the cluster will NOT form.

items.label: Node 0 ETCD configuration (/etc/default/etcd)
items.code:
sudo nano /etc/default/etcd

# Paste the following (change IP to your actual Node 0 IP):
ETCD_NAME="node0"
ETCD_DATA_DIR="/var/lib/etcd"
ETCD_LISTEN_PEER_URLS="http://192.168.68.200:2380"
ETCD_LISTEN_CLIENT_URLS="http://192.168.68.200:2379,http://127.0.0.1:2379"
ETCD_ADVERTISE_PEER_URLS="http://192.168.68.200:2380"
ETCD_ADVERTISE_CLIENT_URLS="http://192.168.68.200:2379"
ETCD_INITIAL_ADVERTISE_PEER_URLS="http://192.168.68.200:2380"
ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER="node0=http://192.168.68.200:2380,node1=http://192.168.68.201:2380,node2=http://192.168.68.202:2380"
ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER_TOKEN="pg-ha-etcd"
ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER_STATE="new"

# Explanation:
# ETCD_NAME must match the name in ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER (e.g. 'node0')
# listen-peer-urls: port 2380 for inter-node communication
# listen-client-urls: port 2379 for Patroni to connect
# advertise-*: what we tell other nodes to connect to (must be reachable by all VMs)
# initial-cluster: list of ALL 3 nodes with their peer URLs (identical on each node)
# initial-cluster-state: 'new' for first time, 'existing' for rejoin

subsections.title: Step 3: Configure ETCD on Node 1 (192.168.68.201)
subsections.description: ⚠️ Replace the example IPs with your actual IPs.

items.label: Node 1 ETCD configuration (/etc/default/etcd)
items.code:
sudo nano /etc/default/etcd

# Paste the following (change IP to your actual Node 1 IP):
ETCD_NAME="node1"
ETCD_DATA_DIR="/var/lib/etcd"
ETCD_LISTEN_PEER_URLS="http://192.168.68.201:2380"
ETCD_LISTEN_CLIENT_URLS="http://192.168.68.201:2379,http://127.0.0.1:2379"
ETCD_ADVERTISE_PEER_URLS="http://192.168.68.201:2380"
ETCD_ADVERTISE_CLIENT_URLS="http://192.168.68.201:2379"
ETCD_INITIAL_ADVERTISE_PEER_URLS="http://192.168.68.201:2380"
ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER="node0=http://192.168.68.200:2380,node1=http://192.168.68.201:2380,node2=http://192.168.68.202:2380"
ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER_TOKEN="pg-ha-etcd"
ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER_STATE="new"

# ⚠️ NOTE: ETCD_NAME must be 'node1' (matching the initial-cluster entry)
# The initial-cluster list is IDENTICAL on all 3 nodes

subsections.title: Step 4: Configure ETCD on Node 2 (192.168.68.202)
subsections.description: ⚠️ Replace the example IPs with your actual IPs.

items.label: Node 2 ETCD configuration (/etc/default/etcd)
items.code:
sudo nano /etc/default/etcd

# Paste the following (change IP to your actual Node 2 IP):
ETCD_NAME="node2"
ETCD_DATA_DIR="/var/lib/etcd"
ETCD_LISTEN_PEER_URLS="http://192.168.68.202:2380"
ETCD_LISTEN_CLIENT_URLS="http://192.168.68.202:2379,http://127.0.0.1:2379"
ETCD_ADVERTISE_PEER_URLS="http://192.168.68.202:2380"
ETCD_ADVERTISE_CLIENT_URLS="http://192.168.68.202:2379"
ETCD_INITIAL_ADVERTISE_PEER_URLS="http://192.168.68.202:2380"
ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER="node0=http://192.168.68.200:2380,node1=http://192.168.68.201:2380,node2=http://192.168.68.202:2380"
ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER_TOKEN="pg-ha-etcd"
ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER_STATE="new"

subsections.title: Step 5: Firewall → Start ETCD → Verify Cluster
subsections.description: ⚠️ CRITICAL: Open ports 2379 and 2380 on ALL 3 nodes BEFORE starting ETCD. These steps MUST be done in order.

items.label: Step 5a: Open firewall ports on ALL 3 NODES
items.code:
# ---------- NODE 0 ----------
sudo ufw allow 2379/tcp
sudo ufw allow 2380/tcp
sudo ufw reload
sudo ufw status | grep -E '2379|2380'

# ---------- NODE 1 ----------
sudo ufw allow 2379/tcp
sudo ufw allow 2380/tcp
sudo ufw reload
sudo ufw status | grep -E '2379|2380'

# ---------- NODE 2 ----------
sudo ufw allow 2379/tcp
sudo ufw allow 2380/tcp
sudo ufw reload
sudo ufw status | grep -E '2379|2380'

# Also allow Patroni REST API port (needed later):
sudo ufw allow 8008/tcp
sudo ufw reload

# Expected output: both 2379/tcp and 2380/tcp listed as ALLOW

items.label: Step 5b: Start ETCD on all 3 nodes (within 2 minutes!)
items.code:
# Start all 3 nodes quickly — the cluster forms when they see each other

# ---------- NODE 0 ----------
sudo systemctl enable etcd
sudo systemctl start etcd

# ---------- NODE 1 ----------
sudo systemctl enable etcd
sudo systemctl start etcd

# ---------- NODE 2 ----------
sudo systemctl enable etcd
sudo systemctl start etcd

# ⚠️ If you wait too long between starting nodes, the first node will time out
# and form a single-node cluster. Stop, delete data, and retry if that happens.

items.label: Step 5c: Verify ETCD is listening on the right IP (not just 127.0.0.1)
items.code:
# On each node, check that ETCD bound to your VM's IP:
ss -tulpn | grep etcd

# GOOD - Node 0 example:
# tcp  LISTEN  192.168.68.200:2379   0.0.0.0:*   users:(("etcd",pid=1234))
# tcp  LISTEN  192.168.68.200:2380   0.0.0.0:*   users:(("etcd",pid=1234))
# tcp  LISTEN  127.0.0.1:2379       0.0.0.0:*   users:(("etcd",pid=1234))
#
# BAD - only shows 127.0.0.1:2379, no external IP
# This means ETCD is NOT reading your config - check /etc/default/etcd
#
# Also verify the ETCD name:
ps aux | grep etcd | grep -v grep
# Expected output includes: etcd --name=node0 (or node1/node2)

items.label: ❗ Fix: ETCD cluster didn't form (only see localhost or single node)
text: If Step 5d shows only 1 endpoint (localhost) instead of 3, the cluster failed to form. Common causes:
Cause 1: Wrong config file - ETCD is using defaults instead of your config
→ Run: systemctl cat etcd | grep EnvironmentFile
→ Make sure /etc/default/etcd has the correct env vars
→ Run: cat /etc/default/etcd | grep ETCD_NAME
Cause 2: Port 2380 blocked between VMs
→ Run: nc -zv node1.local 2380 (from node0)
→ If 'Connection refused': check ufw, iptables, and cloud firewall rules
Cause 3: ETCD_NAME doesn't match the name in ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER
→ The ETCD_NAME in /etc/default/etcd must match the name in ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER
→ E.g. on node0: ETCD_NAME='node0' AND initial-cluster has 'node0=http://...'
Cause 4: Stale data from a previous failed attempt — reset everything:
1. sudo systemctl stop etcd (ALL 3 nodes)
2. sudo rm -rf /var/lib/etcd/* (ALL 3 nodes - clears bad state)
3. Verify /etc/default/etcd has correct IPs on each node
4. sudo systemctl start etcd (ALL 3 nodes, within 2 minutes)
5. Verify: etcdctl endpoint health --cluster --write-out=table

sections.title: Part 2: PostgreSQL Installation on All Nodes
content: Install PostgreSQL 18 on all three nodes from the official PostgreSQL apt repository.

items.label: Run this on ALL 3 NODES
items.code:
# ---------- NODE 0 ----------
sudo apt install -y postgresql-common
sudo sh /usr/share/postgresql-common/pgdg/apt.postgresql.org.sh
sudo apt install postgresql-18 postgresql-client-18 -y
psql --version

# ---------- NODE 1 ----------
sudo apt install -y postgresql-common
sudo sh /usr/share/postgresql-common/pgdg/apt.postgresql.org.sh
sudo apt install postgresql-18 postgresql-client-18 -y
psql --version

# ---------- NODE 2 ----------
sudo apt install -y postgresql-common
sudo sh /usr/share/postgresql-common/pgdg/apt.postgresql.org.sh
sudo apt install postgresql-18 postgresql-client-18 -y
psql --version

# Expected: psql (PostgreSQL) 18.x

subsections.title: Create Data Directories
subsections.content: Patroni will initialize PostgreSQL inside /data/patroni. Create the directories on all nodes now.

items.label: Run this on ALL 3 NODES
items.code:
# ---------- NODE 0 ----------
sudo mkdir -p /data/patroni
sudo mkdir -p /data/pg/{cluster1,cluster2,cluster3}
sudo chown -R postgres:postgres /data
sudo chmod 700 /data/patroni
sudo chmod 700 /data/pg/*

# ---------- NODE 1 ----------
sudo mkdir -p /data/patroni
sudo mkdir -p /data/pg/{cluster1,cluster2,cluster3}
sudo chown -R postgres:postgres /data
sudo chmod 700 /data/patroni
sudo chmod 700 /data/pg/*

# ---------- NODE 2 ----------
sudo mkdir -p /data/patroni
sudo mkdir -p /data/pg/{cluster1,cluster2,cluster3}
sudo chown -R postgres:postgres /data
sudo chmod 700 /data/patroni
sudo chmod 700 /data/pg/*

sections.title: Part 3: Patroni Installation
content: Patroni is a Python application. We'll install it system-wide with pip.

items.label: Run on ALL 3 NODES: Python dependencies
items.code:
# ---------- NODE 0 ----------
sudo apt install python3 python3-pip python3-venv libpq-dev -y
sudo pip3 install psycopg2-binary

# ---------- NODE 1 ----------
sudo apt install python3 python3-pip python3-venv libpq-dev -y
sudo pip3 install psycopg2-binary

# ---------- NODE 2 ----------
sudo apt install python3 python3-pip python3-venv libpq-dev -y
sudo pip3 install psycopg2-binary

items.label: Run on ALL 3 NODES: Install Patroni
items.code:
# ---------- NODE 0 ----------
sudo pip3 install patroni[etcd3]
patroni --version

# ---------- NODE 1 ----------
sudo pip3 install patroni[etcd3]
patroni --version

# ---------- NODE 2 ----------
sudo pip3 install patroni[etcd3]
patroni --version

# Expected: patroni 4.x.x

items.label: Run on ALL 3 NODES: Create Patroni system user
items.code:
# ---------- NODE 0 ----------
sudo useradd --system --no-create-home --shell /bin/false patroni
sudo usermod -aG postgres patroni

# ---------- NODE 1 ----------
sudo useradd --system --no-create-home --shell /bin/false patroni
sudo usermod -aG postgres patroni

# ---------- NODE 2 ----------
sudo useradd --system --no-create-home --shell /bin/false patroni
sudo usermod -aG postgres patroni

sections.title: Part 4: Patroni Configuration
content: Now we configure Patroni to manage PostgreSQL on all 3 nodes. Since each node is a separate VM, each runs PostgreSQL on its own IP and port 5432. Patroni uses ETCD to coordinate leader election and replication.

subsections.title: Create Config Directory on All Nodes

items.label: Run on ALL 3 NODES
items.code:
# ---------- NODE 0 ----------
sudo mkdir -p /etc/patroni
sudo chown -R patroni:patroni /etc/patroni

# ---------- NODE 1 ----------
sudo mkdir -p /etc/patroni
sudo chown -R patroni:patroni /etc/patroni

# ---------- NODE 2 ----------
sudo mkdir -p /etc/patroni
sudo chown -R patroni:patroni /etc/patroni

subsections.title: Node 0: Patroni config (192.168.68.200, port 5432)
subsections.content: This node becomes the initial leader.

items.label: Create /etc/patroni/patroni.yml on Node 0
items.code:
sudo nano /etc/patroni/patroni.yml

# ⚠️ All IPs must match YOUR actual VM IPs
scope: pg-ha-cluster
namespace: /db/
name: node0-pg

restapi:
  listen: 192.168.68.200:8008
  connect_address: 192.168.68.200:8008
  authentication:
    username: patroni_api
    password: secure-api-pass-123
  https: false

etcd:
  hosts: 192.168.68.200:2379,192.168.68.201:2379,192.168.68.202:2379

bootstrap:
  dcs:
    ttl: 30
    loop_wait: 10
    retry_timeout: 10
    maximum_lag_on_failover: 1048576
    postgresql:
      use_pg_rewind: true
      use_slots: true
      parameters:
        wal_level: replica
        wal_log_hints: 'on'
        max_wal_senders: 10
        max_replication_slots: 10
        wal_keep_size: 512
        max_connections: 200
        shared_buffers: 1GB
        work_mem: 128MB
        maintenance_work_mem: 256MB
        effective_cache_size: 3GB

  initdb:
  - encoding: UTF8
  - locale: en_US.UTF-8
  - data-checksums

  pg_hba:
  - host replication replicator 192.168.68.0/24 trust
  - host all all 192.168.68.0/24 trust
  - host all all 127.0.0.1/32 trust

postgresql:
  listen: 192.168.68.200:5432
  connect_address: 192.168.68.200:5432
  data_dir: /data/patroni
  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/18/bin
  pgpass: /tmp/pgpass
  authentication:
    replication:
      username: replicator
      password: repl-pass-456
    superuser:
      username: postgres
      password: super-pass-789
    rewind:
      username: rewind_user
      password: rewind-pass-000
  parameters:
    unix_socket_directories: '/var/run/postgresql'
    port: 5432

tags:
  nofailover: false
  noloadbalance: false
  clonefrom: false
  nosync: false

items.label: Create /etc/patroni/patroni.yml on Node 1
items.code:
sudo nano /etc/patroni/patroni.yml

# ⚠️ Replace IPs with YOUR actual IPs
scope: pg-ha-cluster
namespace: /db/
name: node1-pg

restapi:
  listen: 192.168.68.201:8008
  connect_address: 192.168.68.201:8008
  authentication:
    username: patroni_api
    password: secure-api-pass-123
  https: false

etcd:
  hosts: 192.168.68.200:2379,192.168.68.201:2379,192.168.68.202:2379

postgresql:
  listen: 192.168.68.201:5432
  connect_address: 192.168.68.201:5432
  data_dir: /data/patroni
  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/18/bin
  pgpass: /tmp/pgpass
  authentication:
    replication:
      username: replicator
      password: repl-pass-456
    superuser:
      username: postgres
      password: super-pass-789
    rewind:
      username: rewind_user
      password: rewind-pass-000
  parameters:
    unix_socket_directories: '/var/run/postgresql'
    port: 5432

tags:
  nofailover: false
  noloadbalance: false
  clonefrom: false
  nosync: false

items.label: Create /etc/patroni/patroni.yml on Node 2
items.code:
sudo nano /etc/patroni/patroni.yml

# ⚠️ Replace IPs with YOUR actual IPs
scope: pg-ha-cluster
namespace: /db/
name: node2-pg

restapi:
  listen: 192.168.68.202:8008
  connect_address: 192.168.68.202:8008
  authentication:
    username: patroni_api
    password: secure-api-pass-123
  https: false

etcd:
  hosts: 192.168.68.200:2379,192.168.68.201:2379,192.168.68.202:2379

postgresql:
  listen: 192.168.68.202:5432
  connect_address: 192.168.68.202:5432
  data_dir: /data/patroni
  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/18/bin
  pgpass: /tmp/pgpass
  authentication:
    replication:
      username: replicator
      password: repl-pass-456
    superuser:
      username: postgres
      password: super-pass-789
    rewind:
      username: rewind_user
      password: rewind-pass-000
  parameters:
    unix_socket_directories: '/var/run/postgresql'
    port: 5432

tags:
  nofailover: false
  noloadbalance: false
  clonefrom: false
  nosync: false

rows: Max retry timeout for DCS operations
Max replication lag before promoting replica
Allow former leader to rejoin as replica
Use replication slots to prevent WAL loss
PostgreSQL binary location (adjust for your version)

items.label: Important: PostgreSQL 18 vs 16
text: If you installed PostgreSQL 18, your bin_dir is /usr/lib/postgresql/18/bin.
If you have PostgreSQL 16, change it to /usr/lib/postgresql/16/bin.
If you have PostgreSQL 17, change it to /usr/lib/postgresql/17/bin.
Wrong bin_dir = Patroni crashes with 'Failed to bootstrap cluster' error.
Check your version: pg_config --version

sections.title: Part 5: Starting the Patroni Cluster
content: With all configurations in place, it's time to bootstrap and start the cluster. Start Node 0 first — it initializes the PostgreSQL data directory and becomes the leader. Then start Node 1 and Node 2 — they clone from the leader and become replicas.

subsections.title: Fix /data/patroni permissions BEFORE starting
subsections.content: This is a critical step many guides miss. Patroni runs as user 'patroni' but needs to write to /data/patroni. PostgreSQL runs as user 'postgres'. Both need access.

items.label: Run this on ALL 3 NODES before starting Patroni
items.code:
sudo chown -R patroni:postgres /data/patroni
sudo chmod 750 /data/patroni

# Verify:
ls -la /data/ | grep patroni
# Expected: drwxr-x--- patroni postgres

subsections.title: Stop Existing PostgreSQL Service (avoids port conflicts)

items.label: Stop system PostgreSQL on ALL 3 NODES
items.code:
# ---------- NODE 0 ----------
sudo systemctl stop postgresql
sudo systemctl disable postgresql
sudo systemctl mask postgresql

# ---------- NODE 1 ----------
sudo systemctl stop postgresql
sudo systemctl disable postgresql
sudo systemctl mask postgresql

# ---------- NODE 2 ----------
sudo systemctl stop postgresql
sudo systemctl disable postgresql
sudo systemctl mask postgresql

# Verify: sudo ss -tulpn | grep 5432 (should show nothing)

subsections.title: Create Systemd Service for Patroni (All Nodes)

items.label: Create the same service file on ALL 3 NODES
items.code:
sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/patroni.service

[Unit]
Description=Patroni: PostgreSQL High Availability
After=etcd.service
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=patroni
Group=patroni
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/patroni /etc/patroni/patroni.yml
KillMode=process
Restart=always
RestartSec=10
TimeoutSec=30

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

# Create the identical file on ALL 3 NODES

items.label: Start Node 0 FIRST (becomes leader), then Nodes 1 & 2
items.code:
# ⚠️ IMPORTANT ORDER: Node 0 → wait 30s → Node 1 → Node 2

# ---------- NODE 0 (LEADER) ----------
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable patroni
sudo systemctl start patroni

# Wait 30 seconds for Node 0 to bootstrap:
sudo journalctl -u patroni -n 30 --no-pager
# Look for: "no action. I am (node0-pg), the leader with the lock"

# ---------- NODE 1 ----------
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable patroni
sudo systemctl start patroni

# ---------- NODE 2 ----------
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable patroni
sudo systemctl start patroni

items.label: Check cluster with patronictl (run on any node)
items.code:
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml list

# Expected output:
# + Cluster: pg-ha-cluster (7641578443030348846) ---+----+-------------+-----+
# | Member   | Host           | Role    | State     | TL | ... |
# +----------+----------------+---------+-----------+----+-----+
# | node0-pg | 192.168.68.200 | Leader  | running   |  1 |     |
# | node1-pg | 192.168.68.201 | Replica | streaming |  1 |     |
# | node2-pg | 192.168.68.202 | Replica | streaming |  1 |     |
# +----------+----------------+---------+-----------+----+-----+

# If a node shows 'creating replica' or 'start failed', wait 30s and retry.

items.label: Check via Patroni REST API

sections.title: Part 6: Working with patronictl
content: patronictl is the command-line tool for managing and monitoring your Patroni cluster.

items.label: Manual switchover (graceful leader change)
items.code:
# Promote node1-pg to leader, demote node0-pg to replica:
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml switchover --master node0-pg --candidate node1-pg --scheduled now

# Type 'y' to confirm

# Verify:
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml list
# Expected: node1-pg is now Leader, node0-pg is Replica

items.label: View cluster history and config
items.code:
# Show cluster history (timeline changes):
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml history

# Show current DCS configuration:
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml show-config

items.label: Reinitialize a failed replica
items.code:
# If node2-pg is corrupted or has replication issues:
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml reinit pg-ha-cluster node2-pg

# This destroys the node's data directory and re-clones from the leader

sections.title: Part 7: HAProxy Load Balancer Setup
content: HAProxy routes application traffic to the correct PostgreSQL node. Write queries go to the leader, read queries are distributed across all replicas. HAProxy uses Patroni's REST API (port 8008) to dynamically detect which node is the leader.
Install HAProxy on ONE node only — we recommend Node 0 (192.168.68.200). This becomes your single entry point for all database traffic.

items.label: Install on Node 0
items.code:
node0:~$ sudo apt install haproxy -y
node0:~$ sudo systemctl stop haproxy

subsections.title: Configure HAProxy (edit config on Node 0)
subsections.content: The HAProxy config file is located at /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg on Node 0. Edit this file and replace its contents with the configuration below.

items.label: Edit /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg on Node 0
items.code:
sudo nano /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg

# ⚠️ Delete the existing content and paste this:

global
    maxconn 1000
    log 127.0.0.1 local2
    stats socket /var/run/haproxy.sock mode 600 level admin
    stats timeout 30s

defaults
    mode tcp
    log global
    option tcplog
    timeout connect 5000ms
    timeout client 50000ms
    timeout server 50000ms
    timeout check 2000ms

# HAProxy Statistics Dashboard (port 7000)
listen stats
    mode http
    bind *:7000
    stats enable
    stats uri /stats
    stats refresh 10s
    stats auth admin:admin-pass-123

# PostgreSQL Write (Primary) - Port 5000
listen pg-write
    bind *:5000
    mode tcp
    option httpchk GET /primary
    http-check expect status 200
    default-server inter 3s fall 3 rise 2 on-marked-down shutdown-sessions
    
    server node0-pg 192.168.68.200:5432 check port 8008
    server node1-pg 192.168.68.201:5432 check port 8008
    server node2-pg 192.168.68.202:5432 check port 8008

# PostgreSQL Read (Replicas) - Port 5001
listen pg-read
    bind *:5001
    mode tcp
    balance roundrobin
    option httpchk GET /replica
    http-check expect status 200
    default-server inter 3s fall 3 rise 2
    
    server node0-pg 192.168.68.200:5432 check port 8008
    server node1-pg 192.168.68.201:5432 check port 8008
    server node2-pg 192.168.68.202:5432 check port 8008

items.label: Health check logic
text: HAProxy checks every Patroni node on port 8008 (Patroni's REST API) every 3 seconds:
• Write port (5000) → HAProxy sends GET /primary to each node
- Only the leader returns HTTP 200 → writes always go to leader
- Replicas return HTTP 503 → they are skipped for writes
• Read port (5001) → HAProxy sends GET /replica to each node
- Replicas return HTTP 200 → reads load-balanced across replicas
- Leader also returns HTTP 200 on /replica (acts as replica for reads too)
If a node fails 3 consecutive health checks, HAProxy marks it down
and stops sending traffic. When it recovers, HAProxy auto-detects.
If the leader fails, Patroni promotes a replica within 10-30 seconds,
and HAProxy detects the new leader via the /primary health check.

items.label: Enable, start, and verify HAProxy
items.code:
node0:~$ sudo systemctl enable haproxy
node0:~$ sudo systemctl start haproxy
node0:~$ sudo systemctl status haproxy
# Expected: Active: active (running)

# Check the HAProxy stats:
curl -u admin:admin-pass-123 http://localhost:7000/stats

subsections.title: IMPORTANT: Open Port 8008 on ALL 3 Nodes
subsections.content: HAProxy checks Patroni health via port 8008. If this port is blocked, all nodes show DOWN.

items.label: Run this on ALL 3 NODES
items.code:
sudo ufw allow 8008/tcp
sudo ufw reload

# Verify HAProxy health checks work:
curl -s http://192.168.68.200:8008/replica
curl -s http://192.168.68.201:8008/replica
curl -s http://192.168.68.202:8008/replica

# All should return HTTP 200 (even the leader returns 200 on /replica)

items.label: Connect via HAProxy write port (5000) → always goes to leader
items.code:
# Connect through HAProxy on Node 0:
psql -h 192.168.68.200 -p 5000 -U postgres -d postgres

# After entering password:
postgres=# SELECT inet_server_addr(), inet_server_port();
# Expected: shows the leader's IP (e.g. 192.168.68.200) and port 5432

postgres=# CREATE DATABASE test_ha;
CREATE DATABASE
postgres=# \q

items.label: Connect via HAProxy read port (5001) → load-balanced to replicas
items.code:
# Connect through HAProxy:
psql -h 192.168.68.200 -p 5001 -U postgres -d postgres

postgres=# SELECT inet_server_addr(), inet_server_port();
# Expected: shows a replica's IP (e.g. 192.168.68.201) and port 5432
# Run twice — with round-robin you'll see different replicas

postgres=# \q

sections.title: Part 8: Testing High Availability (Failover)
content: The most important test of any HA system is simulating a failure and verifying automatic recovery.

items.label: Test 1: Stop the Leader Process
items.code:
# Simulate leader failure by stopping Patroni on the leader:
sudo systemctl stop patroni

# On another node, check the cluster:
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml list

# Expected: one of the replicas was auto-promoted to Leader!
# + Cluster: pg-ha-cluster ----------+---------+----+-----------+
# | Member   | Host           | Role    | State     | TL | Lag |
# +----------+----------------+---------+-----------+----+-----+
# | node0-pg | 192.168.68.200 | unknown | stopped   |  1 |     |
# | node1-pg | 192.168.68.201 | Leader  | running   |  2 |     |
# | node2-pg | 192.168.68.202 | Replica | streaming |  2 |   0 |
# +----------+----------------+---------+-----------+----+-----+

# Timeline advanced from 1 to 2 (new leader epoch)

items.label: Test 2: Bring the Failed Node Back
items.code:
# Start Patroni on the failed node:
sudo systemctl start patroni

# Check cluster:
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml list

# Expected: old leader rejoins as Replica (not Leader)!
# + Cluster: pg-ha-cluster ----------+---------+----+-----------+
# | Member   | Host           | Role    | State     | TL | Lag |
# +----------+----------------+---------+-----------+----+-----+
# | node0-pg | 192.168.68.200 | Replica | streaming |  2 |   0 |
# | node1-pg | 192.168.68.201 | Leader  | running   |  2 |     |
# | node2-pg | 192.168.68.202 | Replica | streaming |  2 |   0 |
# +----------+----------------+---------+-----------+----+-----+

# Patroni auto-detected the old leader and re-joined it as a replica

items.label: Test 3: Kill PostgreSQL Directly (Patroni auto-restarts it)
items.code:
# Kill PostgreSQL on the current leader (node1):
sudo kill -9 $(head -n 1 /data/patroni/postmaster.pid)

# Wait 10 seconds (Patroni's loop_wait) and check:
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml list

# Expected: Patroni restarted PostgreSQL on node1 within seconds
# If restart fails repeatedly, Patroni marks the node as failed
# and promotes a replica. Check Patroni logs:
sudo journalctl -u patroni -n 20 --no-pager

items.label: Test 4: Simulate Network Partition
items.code:
# Block ETCD client port on the leader:
sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 2379 -j DROP

# After ~30 seconds (ttl), the leader can't renew its lock in ETCD
# Replicas notice the leader lock is gone and hold a new election

# Restore connectivity:
sudo iptables -D INPUT -p tcp --dport 2379 -j DROP

# Check cluster:
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml list

sections.title: Part 9: Running Multiple HA Clusters on Different Ports
content: In production, you often need to run multiple PostgreSQL clusters side by side — different applications, environments, or tenants. Each cluster needs its own scope name in ETCD, its own PostgreSQL ports, and its own Patroni config file.
Since each VM already uses port 5432 for Cluster A, Cluster B uses ports 5442, 5443, 5444 (one per node) with a different REST API port (8009).

subsections.title: Two Separate HA Clusters Layout
subsections.content: We'll create two independent HA clusters on the same 3 nodes:

subsections.title: Cluster B: Node 0 Config (Port 5442, REST API 8009)

items.label: Create /etc/patroni/patroni-cluster-b.yml on Node 0
items.code:
sudo nano /etc/patroni/patroni-cluster-b.yml

# ⚠️ Replace IPs with your actual IPs
scope: pg-cluster-b
namespace: /db/
name: node0-cluster-b

restapi:
  listen: 192.168.68.200:8009
  connect_address: 192.168.68.200:8009
  authentication:
    username: patroni_api
    password: secure-api-pass-123

etcd:
  hosts: 192.168.68.200:2379,192.168.68.201:2379,192.168.68.202:2379

bootstrap:
  dcs:
    ttl: 30
    loop_wait: 10
    retry_timeout: 10
    maximum_lag_on_failover: 1048576
    postgresql:
      use_pg_rewind: true
      use_slots: true
      parameters:
        wal_level: replica
        wal_log_hints: 'on'
        max_wal_senders: 10
        max_replication_slots: 10
        wal_keep_size: 512
        max_connections: 100
        shared_buffers: 512MB

  initdb:
  - encoding: UTF8
  - locale: en_US.UTF-8
  - data-checksums

  pg_hba:
  - host replication replicator_b 192.168.68.0/24 trust
  - host all all 192.168.68.0/24 trust

postgresql:
  listen: 192.168.68.200:5442
  connect_address: 192.168.68.200:5442
  data_dir: /data/pg/cluster_b/node0
  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/18/bin
  pgpass: /tmp/pgpass-cluster-b
  authentication:
    replication:
      username: replicator_b
      password: repl-b-pass-456
    superuser:
      username: postgres
      password: super-b-pass-789
    rewind:
      username: rewind_user_b
      password: rewind-b-pass-000
  parameters:
    port: 5442

tags:
  nofailover: false
  noloadbalance: false
  clonefrom: false
  nosync: false

subsections.title: Cluster B: Node 1 Config (Port 5443, REST API 8009)

items.label: Create /etc/patroni/patroni-cluster-b.yml on Node 1
items.code:
sudo nano /etc/patroni/patroni-cluster-b.yml

scope: pg-cluster-b
namespace: /db/
name: node1-cluster-b

restapi:
  listen: 192.168.68.201:8009
  connect_address: 192.168.68.201:8009

etcd:
  hosts: 192.168.68.200:2379,192.168.68.201:2379,192.168.68.202:2379

postgresql:
  listen: 192.168.68.201:5443
  connect_address: 192.168.68.201:5443
  data_dir: /data/pg/cluster_b/node1
  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/18/bin
  authentication:
    replication:
      username: replicator_b
      password: repl-b-pass-456
    superuser:
      username: postgres
      password: super-b-pass-789
    rewind:
      username: rewind_user_b
      password: rewind-b-pass-000
  parameters:
    port: 5443

tags:
  nofailover: false
  noloadbalance: false
  clonefrom: false
  nosync: false

subsections.title: Cluster B: Node 2 Config (Port 5444, REST API 8009)

items.label: Create /etc/patroni/patroni-cluster-b.yml on Node 2
items.code:
sudo nano /etc/patroni/patroni-cluster-b.yml

scope: pg-cluster-b
namespace: /db/
name: node2-cluster-b

restapi:
  listen: 192.168.68.202:8009
  connect_address: 192.168.68.202:8009

etcd:
  hosts: 192.168.68.200:2379,192.168.68.201:2379,192.168.68.202:2379

postgresql:
  listen: 192.168.68.202:5444
  connect_address: 192.168.68.202:5444
  data_dir: /data/pg/cluster_b/node2
  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/18/bin
  authentication:
    replication:
      username: replicator_b
      password: repl-b-pass-456
    superuser:
      username: postgres
      password: super-b-pass-789
    rewind:
      username: rewind_user_b
      password: rewind-b-pass-000
  parameters:
    port: 5444

tags:
  nofailover: false
  noloadbalance: false
  clonefrom: false
  nosync: false

subsections.title: Create Data Directories for Cluster B

items.label: Create directories on ALL 3 NODES
items.code:
# ---------- NODE 0 ----------
sudo mkdir -p /data/pg/cluster_b/node0
sudo chown -R patroni:postgres /data/pg/cluster_b

# ---------- NODE 1 ----------
sudo mkdir -p /data/pg/cluster_b/node1
sudo chown -R patroni:postgres /data/pg/cluster_b

# ---------- NODE 2 ----------
sudo mkdir -p /data/pg/cluster_b/node2
sudo chown -R patroni:postgres /data/pg/cluster_b

items.label: Create systemd service on ALL 3 NODES, then start (Node 0 first)
items.code:
# Create the same service file on ALL 3 NODES:
sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/patroni-cluster-b.service

[Unit]
Description=Patroni Cluster B: PostgreSQL HA
After=etcd.service
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=patroni
Group=patroni
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/patroni /etc/patroni/patroni-cluster-b.yml
KillMode=process
Restart=always
RestartSec=10

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

# Enable & start (Node 0 FIRST):
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable patroni-cluster-b
sudo systemctl start patroni-cluster-b

# Then Nodes 1 and 2:
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable patroni-cluster-b
sudo systemctl start patroni-cluster-b

items.label: Check both clusters simultaneously
items.code:
# Cluster A:
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml list

# Cluster B:
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni-cluster-b.yml list

# Both should show 3 members with one Leader and two Replicas

items.label: Add Cluster B to /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg on Node 0
items.code:
sudo nano /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg

# Add these sections AFTER the existing Cluster A config:

# Cluster B Write
listen pg-cluster-b-write
    bind *:6000
    mode tcp
    option httpchk GET /primary
    http-check expect status 200
    default-server inter 3s fall 3 rise 2 on-marked-down shutdown-sessions
    server node0-cluster-b 192.168.68.200:5442 check port 8009
    server node1-cluster-b 192.168.68.201:5443 check port 8009
    server node2-cluster-b 192.168.68.202:5444 check port 8009

# Cluster B Read
listen pg-cluster-b-read
    bind *:6001
    mode tcp
    balance roundrobin
    option httpchk GET /replica
    http-check expect status 200
    default-server inter 3s fall 3 rise 2
    server node0-cluster-b 192.168.68.200:5442 check port 8009
    server node1-cluster-b 192.168.68.201:5443 check port 8009
    server node2-cluster-b 192.168.68.202:5444 check port 8009

# Reload HAProxy:
sudo systemctl reload haproxy

# Test both clusters:
psql -h localhost -p 5000 -U postgres -d postgres -c "SELECT 'Cluster A Write OK'"
psql -h localhost -p 6000 -U postgres -d postgres -c "SELECT 'Cluster B Write OK'"

sections.title: Part 10: Monitoring Your HA Cluster
content: Monitoring is essential for any production deployment. Patroni provides multiple ways to check your cluster's health.

subsections.title: Patroni REST API Endpoints
subsections.content: Each Patroni node exposes a REST API on port 8008 (or 8009 for Cluster B).

items.label: Useful API endpoints
items.code:
# Cluster info (shows all members):
curl -s http://192.168.68.200:8008/cluster | python3 -m json.tool

# Node health:
curl -s http://192.168.68.200:8008/health | python3 -m json.tool
# Returns: {"state":"running","role":"primary"} or "replica"

# Database server info:
curl -s http://192.168.68.200:8008/ | python3 -m json.tool

# Patroni version and internal state:
curl -s http://192.168.68.200:8008/patroni | python3 -m json.tool

subsections.title: HAProxy Statistics Dashboard
subsections.content: Open this in your browser for a live view of all backends.

items.label: Access HAProxy dashboard (from Node 0)
items.code:
# Open in browser:
http://192.168.68.200:7000/stats

# Login: admin / admin-pass-123

# Or check via command line:
curl -u admin:admin-pass-123 http://localhost:7000/stats | grep -E "pg-|UP|DOWN"

items.label: Watch cluster status in real time
items.code:
watch -n 2 'sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml list'

# This refreshes every 2 seconds showing role changes and replication lag

items.label: Check replication status (run on the leader)
items.code:
-- On the leader:
SELECT client_addr, state, sync_state, 
       pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), replay_lsn)) AS lag
FROM pg_stat_replication;

-- Expected: both replicas show 'streaming' state, 0 bytes lag

-- Check replication slots:
SELECT slot_name, slot_type, active, restart_lsn FROM pg_replication_slots;

sections.title: Part 11: Backup & Point-in-Time Recovery
content: High availability protects against server failures, but you still need backups for data corruption, accidental deletions, and disasters.

infoBox.content: HA is NOT a backup! Replication protects against server failure. Backups protect against data corruption, accidental DROP TABLE, and ransomware. You need both.

subsections.title: Enable WAL Archiving
subsections.content: WAL archiving is required for point-in-time recovery (PITR). Configure it via Patroni's DCS config.

items.label: Update Patroni DCS config for WAL archiving
items.code:
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml edit-config

# Add these parameters:
postgresql:
  parameters:
    archive_mode: 'on'
    archive_command: 'pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha archive-push %p'
    archive_timeout: 60

# Or use the command-line update:
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml update-config --apply - <<EOF
postgresql:
  parameters:
    archive_mode: 'on'
    archive_command: 'pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha archive-push %p'
    archive_timeout: 60
EOF

items.label: Install pgBackRest (on the leader node)
items.code: sudo apt install pgbackrest -y

items.label: Configure pgBackRest
items.code:
sudo nano /etc/pgbackrest/pgbackrest.conf

[pg-ha]
pg1-path=/data/patroni
pg1-port=5432
pg1-user=postgres

[global]
repo1-path=/backup/pgbackrest
repo1-retention-full=4
repo1-retention-diff=7
repo1-cipher-type=none
process-max=4
compress-type=gz

[global:archive-push]
compress-level=6

items.label: Create stanza and take first backup
items.code:
sudo mkdir -p /backup/pgbackrest
sudo chown -R postgres:postgres /backup
sudo -u postgres pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha stanza-create

# Take a full backup:
sudo -u postgres pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha --type=full backup

# Verify backup:
sudo -u postgres pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha info

# Expected: shows a full backup with timestamp and size

items.label: Restore to a specific point in time
items.code:
# Stop the cluster:
sudo systemctl stop patroni

# Restore from backup to a specific time:
sudo -u postgres pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha --type=time \
  --target="2026-05-19 09:30:00" --target-action=promote restore

# Restart Patroni (it will detect restored data):
sudo systemctl start patroni

items.label: Daily cron job (run on the leader)
items.code:
sudo crontab -e

# Full backup every Sunday at 2 AM
0 2 * * 0 /usr/bin/pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha --type=full backup >> /var/log/pgbackrest-full.log 2>&1

# Differential backup every day at 3 AM (except Sunday)
0 3 * * 1-6 /usr/bin/pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha --type=diff backup >> /var/log/pgbackrest-diff.log 2>&1

# Verify backups weekly
0 4 * * 0 /usr/bin/pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha check

sections.title: Troubleshooting
sections.description: Common issues you'll encounter and how to fix them. Based on real-world experience.

subsections.title: ETCD Shows 'Name: ubuntu' and Only Listens on 127.0.0.1
subsections.content: This is the #1 most common ETCD issue. The service is ignoring your config.

items.label: Root Cause & Fix
text: Symptom: systemctl status etcd shows 'Name: ubuntu' and 'serving insecure client requests on 127.0.0.1:2379'
Cause: Ubuntu's apt etcd package reads config from /etc/default/etcd (environment variables),
NOT from /etc/etcd/etcd.conf.yml. If you created a YAML file, it's being ignored.
Fix: Check which config file the service actually uses:
systemctl cat etcd | grep EnvironmentFile
If it shows EnvironmentFile=-/etc/default/etcd, set env vars in /etc/default/etcd.
After fixing: sudo systemctl daemon-reload; sudo systemctl restart etcd
Verify: ss -tulpn | grep etcd (should show your VM's IP)

subsections.title: ETCD Cluster Won't Form (Only localhost Visible)
subsections.content: You see only one endpoint with localhost:2379 instead of 3 nodes.

items.label: Diagnostic Steps
items.code:
# 1. Check each node's config:
cat /etc/default/etcd | grep -E 'ETCD_NAME|ETCD_LISTEN|ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER'
# Verify: ETCD_NAME matches the name in ETCD_INITIAL_CLUSTER
# Verify: listen URLs use the actual VM IP, not 127.0.0.1

# 2. Check if ETCD is bound to the right IP:
ss -tulpn | grep etcd

# 3. Reset and retry:
sudo systemctl stop etcd
sudo rm -rf /var/lib/etcd/*
# Verify config, then:
sudo systemctl start etcd

subsections.title: Patroni Fails with 'Permission denied: /data/patroni'
subsections.content: The patroni user can't access the data directory.

items.label: Fix permissions
items.code:
sudo chown -R patroni:postgres /data/patroni
sudo chmod 750 /data/patroni
sudo systemctl restart patroni

# Verify: ls -la /data/ | grep patroni
# Should show: drwxr-x--- patroni postgres

subsections.title: Patroni Fails with 'Failed to bootstrap cluster'
subsections.content: Usually caused by wrong bin_dir (PostgreSQL version mismatch).

items.label: Fix PostgreSQL version in config
items.code:
# Check your PostgreSQL version:
pg_config --version
ls /usr/lib/postgresql/

# Update /etc/patroni/patroni.yml with the correct bin_dir:
# For PG 18:  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/18/bin
# For PG 16:  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/16/bin
# For PG 17:  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/17/bin

# After fixing:
sudo systemctl restart patroni

subsections.title: Split Brain (Two Leaders Detected)
subsections.content: Rare but possible during network failures.

items.label: Fix split brain
items.code:
# Identify which node has the most recent data (higher timeline)
# Stop Patroni on the incorrect leader:
sudo systemctl stop patroni

# Restart it (it will rejoin as replica):
sudo systemctl start patroni

# Verify:
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml list

subsections.title: HAProxy Shows All Nodes DOWN (L4TOUT)
subsections.content: HAProxy can't reach Patroni's REST API on port 8008.

items.label: Open port 8008 on all nodes
items.code:
# Run on ALL 3 nodes:
sudo ufw allow 8008/tcp
sudo ufw reload

# Verify from Node 0:
curl -s http://192.168.68.200:8008/health
curl -s http://192.168.68.201:8008/health
curl -s http://192.168.68.202:8008/health

# All should return JSON with "state":"running"

# Reload HAProxy:
sudo systemctl reload haproxy

# Check HAProxy stats:
echo "show stat" | sudo socat stdio /var/run/haproxy.sock | grep pg-

subsections.title: Replication Lag
subsections.content: High lag can cause data loss during failover.

items.label: Check and fix replication lag
items.code:
-- Check lag on leader:
SELECT application_name, state, 
       pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), replay_lsn)) AS lag
FROM pg_stat_replication;

-- Common causes:
-- 1. Slow network → check ping times between nodes
-- 2. Under-provisioned replicas → add CPU/RAM
-- 3. Long-running queries on replicas → check pg_stat_activity
-- 4. wal_keep_size too small → increase to 1024 or higher

items.label: Diagnose startup failure
items.code:
# Check logs:
sudo journalctl -u patroni -n 100 --no-pager

# Common issues:
# - Port already in use: ss -tulpn | grep -E '5432|8008'
# - Data directory permissions: ls -la /data/patroni
# - Wrong bin_dir: cat /etc/patroni/patroni.yml | grep bin_dir
# - Python dependency: pip3 list | grep patroni

# Fix permissions:
sudo chown -R patroni:postgres /data/patroni

# Validate config:
sudo -u patroni patroni /etc/patroni/patroni.yml --validate

sections.title: Summary & Best Practices
content: You've built a complete PostgreSQL High Availability cluster with Patroni, ETCD, and HAProxy. Let's recap.

rows: Distributed key-value store for leader election

items.label: Production Checklist
text: ✓ Minimum 3 nodes for reliable quorum (5 for large deployments)
✓ Regular failover testing (at least once a month)
✓ Monitor replication lag (alert if > 100MB)
✓ Enable WAL archiving and backup automation
✓ Set up Patroni REST API authentication
✓ Use separate ports for different clusters
✓ Configure alerts for node failures
✓ Document your failover procedures
✓ Keep PostgreSQL version consistent across all nodes

items.label: Final Architecture Diagram
text: │              APPLICATION LAYER                               │
│    Web Apps • API Services • Microservices                  │
│       HAPROXY LOAD BALANCER (Node 0 - 192.168.68.200)       │
│    Write: Port 5000 → Leader    Read: Port 5001 → Replicas  │
│ Node 0      │    │ Node 1       │    │ Node 2          │
│ PostgreSQL  │    │ PostgreSQL   │    │ PostgreSQL      │
│ Patroni     │    │ Patroni      │    │ Patroni         │
│ ETCD        │    │ ETCD         │    │ ETCD            │
│      ETCD CLUSTER           │
│  Leader Election + Config   │

infoBox.content: Always test your failover procedures in a staging environment before deploying to production. Automate failover tests using tools like Chaos Engineering or LitmusChaos to build confidence in your HA setup.

items.label: Quick Commands Reference
items.code:
# Cluster status
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml list

# ETCD health
sudo ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint health --cluster

# Check which config ETCD reads
systemctl cat etcd | grep EnvironmentFile

# View ETCD config
cat /etc/default/etcd

# Patroni logs
sudo journalctl -u patroni -f

# ETCD logs
sudo journalctl -u etcd -n 100

# HAProxy logs
sudo tail -f /var/log/haproxy.log

# Restart services in order
sudo systemctl restart etcd
sudo systemctl restart patroni
sudo systemctl reload haproxy
